lesson.name: Schopenhauer: The World as Will - Lesson 2 — Studies in Pessimism

meta.lessonObjective: Suffering, denial of the will, and meaning.

tasks.chapterTitle: On the Futility and Suffering of Life
Reflections on Existence and Human Nature
Intellect, Sympathy, and Genuine Creative Work
Imagination, Memory, and the Progression of Knowledge
Human Reason, Species Preservation, and Gender Perspectives
On Noise, Physical Stature, and Social Distance

messages.content:
Lesson 2 — Studies in Pessimism - Presentation24 Slides
Suffering as Life's True Object
  • Unless suffering is the direct and immediate object of life, our existence must entirely fail of its aim.
  • It is absurd to look upon the enormous amount of pain that abounds everywhere in the world as serving no purpose at all and the result of mere chance.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the text, what is the direct and immediate object of life?
questions.explanation: p.1 states: 'Unless suffering is the direct and immediate object of life, our existence must entirely fail of its aim.'

questions.question: How does Schopenhauer describe evil in contrast to most philosophical systems?
options: Evil is positive and makes its own existence felt
Evil is an illusion of the intellect
Evil is balanced by equal good
questions.explanation: p.1: 'Evil is just what is positive; it makes its own existence felt.'

questions.question: What does the text identify as the only true title every man possesses without question?
questions.explanation: p.9: 'there is nothing in the world to which every man has a more unassailable title than to his own life and person.'

questions.question: Why does the text say a ship without ballast will not go straight?
options: It lacks a certain amount of care or pain or trouble
questions.explanation: p.1 uses the image to show necessity of suffering.

questions.question: What element does the text say we should give play to in order to rise above the common will?
questions.explanation: p.14: 'in every manifestation of self we should, as far as possible, give play to the intellect alone.'

cards.front: What must be the object of life if existence is not to fail of its aim?
cards.back: Suffering must be the direct and immediate object of life.

cards.front: Is evil negative according to Schopenhauer?
cards.back: No, evil is positive; it makes its own existence felt.

cards.front: What is good described as?
cards.back: Good is negative; happiness implies some pain brought to an end.

cards.front: What image illustrates humanity's relation to fate?
cards.back: We are like lambs under the eye of the butcher.

cards.front: What happens when time stays its hand?
cards.back: We are delivered over to the misery of boredom.

cards.front: Why is a certain amount of trouble necessary?
cards.back: A ship without ballast is unstable and will not go straight.

cards.front: What title does every man possess unassailably?
cards.back: His own life and person.

cards.front: How did ancient Stoics view suicide?
cards.back: They praised it as a noble and heroic action when misfortunes are too great.

cards.front: What is the foundation of all being?
cards.back: Will, as the thing-in-itself.

cards.front: Which faculty should dominate to rise above the common will?
cards.back: Intellect and knowledge.

slides.title: Suffering as Life's True Object
bullets: Unless suffering is the direct and immediate object of life, our existence must entirely fail of its aim.
It is absurd to look upon the enormous amount of pain that abounds everywhere in the world as serving no purpose at all and the result of mere chance.
If suffering is refused this central place, every attempt at satisfaction collapses into fresh disappointment because the will keeps demanding what the world cannot stably give.

Only when pain is endured as the very condition of insight does the will turn against itself, opening the sole path by which existence acquires any lasting significance rather than remaining a blind repetition of striving.
slides.notes: Opens the core argument on p.1.

slides.title: Evil as Positive Reality
bullets: I know of no greater absurdity than that propounded by most systems of philosophy in declaring evil to be negative in its character.
Evil is just what is positive; it makes its own existence felt.

This insistence on evil's positive force means that suffering cannot be dismissed as a mere absence of good, but instead asserts itself as an active presence that shapes every moment of willing and desiring.

The result is a direct challenge to any optimistic system: if evil generates its own reality, then meaning can arise only through a radical turning away from the will itself, rather than through attempts to balance or explain suffering away.
slides.notes: Direct refutation of Leibniz on p.1.

slides.title: Good as Mere Negation
bullets: It is the good which is negative; in other words, happiness and satisfaction always imply some desire fulfilled, some state of pain brought to an end.
This explains the fact that we generally find pleasure to be not nearly so pleasant as we expected, and pain very much more painful.
Because every satisfaction merely cancels a preceding want, the will is driven onward without pause, turning life into an unbroken sequence of fresh longings that leave no room for genuine rest.

This purely negative standing of the good therefore exposes the futility of seeking lasting fulfillment in the world and directs attention instead to the quieting of desire itself as the sole route beyond perpetual disappointment.
slides.notes: Explains asymmetry of experience on p.1.

slides.title: Misfortune as the Rule
bullets: Each separate misfortune, as it comes, seems, no doubt, to be something exceptional; but misfortune in general is the rule.
The pleasure in this world, it has been said, outweighs the pain; or, at any rate, there is an even balance between the two.
Once this pattern is seen, every private complaint loses its claim to special exemption and the mind is driven to ask why the will keeps demanding what the world is plainly unable to supply.
The supposed balance of pleasure and pain therefore collapses into a standing deficit that can be closed only by turning against the will rather than negotiating with its cravings.
slides.notes: Counters optimistic claims on p.1.

slides.title: The Image of the Butcher
bullets: If the reader wishes to see shortly whether this statement is true, let him compare the respective feelings of two animals, one of which is engaged in eating the other.
We are like lambs in a field, disporting themselves under the eye of the butcher, who chooses out first one and then another for his prey.

This vision of universal predation shows that every moment of vitality draws its strength from another's destruction, so the will to live sustains itself only by converting the world into an endless chain of torment.

Once we grasp our shared position under the knife, the pursuit of individual happiness appears not merely futile but complicit in the very suffering it seeks to avoid, opening the sole path to meaning through the denial of that will.
slides.notes: Concrete illustration of universal suffering on p.1.

slides.title: Unconsciousness of Future Evil
bullets: So it is that in our good days we are all unconscious of the evil Fate may have presently in store for us—sickness, poverty, mutilation, loss of sight or reason.
No little part of the torment of existence lies in this, that Time is continually pressing upon us, never letting us take breath, but always coming after us, like a taskmaster with a whip.

This blindness therefore lets the will fasten itself more tightly to every passing comfort, so that when fate finally strikes the resulting anguish cuts deeper than any steady expectation could have produced.

Time’s ceaseless advance thus turns our very ignorance into an instrument of torment, ensuring that each new affliction bursts upon us without warning and leaves the will still more desperate for denial.
slides.notes: Time as tormentor on p.1.

slides.title: Boredom When Time Relents
bullets: If at any moment Time stays his hand, it is only when we are delivered over to the misery of boredom.
A certain amount of care or pain or trouble is necessary for every man at all times.

When necessity no longer drives us, the will confronts its own lack of any lasting object and therefore turns the remaining hours into a heavy, colorless expanse that must be killed rather than lived.
The result is that human beings invent fresh cares or distractions simply to escape the revelation that existence without striving is already a form of suffering.
slides.notes: Balances pressure and emptiness on p.1.

slides.title: The Ship Without Ballast
bullets: If the lives of men were relieved of all need, hardship and adversity, they would be so swollen with arrogance that they would present the spectacle of unbridled folly.
A ship without ballast is unstable and will not go straight.
Without that steadying weight the will drives men from one desire to the next, each satisfaction revealing only a fresh void and leaving existence without any inward direction.

Suffering therefore becomes the hidden condition for renunciation: only when the ballast of adversity presses upon us do we glimpse the possibility of turning away from willing altogether and finding meaning in the quiet that follows.
slides.notes: Shows necessity of suffering on p.1.

slides.title: Work as Our Enduring Lot
bullets: Certain it is that work, worry, labor and trouble form the lot of almost all men their whole life long.
If all wishes were fulfilled as soon as they arose, how would men occupy their lives?
Thus the ceaseless demand of labor acts as a blunt instrument that keeps the restless will from collapsing into the heavier torment of boredom, converting raw striving into the dull ache of fatigue.

Yet this arrangement carries its own sharp consequence, since the same activity that fills the hours also regenerates fresh desires, so that any momentary pause only exposes the underlying emptiness work was meant to conceal.
slides.notes: Defines the human condition on p.1.

slides.title: The Comedy Seen from Afar
bullets: Details, as presented, say, in a comedy, how ridiculous it all seems!
It is like a drop of water seen through a microscope, a single drop teeming with infusoria.
From this remote vantage the fierce personal claims that drive each hour lose their weight and reveal themselves as frantic motions inside a sealed bubble whose walls we cannot see while inside it.
The resulting laughter therefore carries a quiet warning that only by loosening our grip on those same claims can the spectacle cease to be our own torment.
slides.notes: Microscopic view of striving on p.9.

slides.title: Life Magnified by Time and Space
bullets: It is an indivisible point, drawn out and magnified by the powerful lenses of Time and Space.
Whether here, or in the little span of human life, this terrible activity produces a comic effect.
The enlargement therefore turns every fleeting pang into an apparently endless drama, binding the will more tightly to existence until the mind pierces the optical illusion and withdraws its assent.

Once the comic disproportion is felt, the search for personal meaning collapses into quiet astonishment, leaving only the possibility of renunciation that lets the magnified image collapse back into its original nothingness.
slides.notes: Explains illusion of importance on p.9.

slides.title: Suicide and the Title to One's Life
bullets: It is quite obvious that there is nothing in the world to which every man has a more unassailable title than to his own life and person.
Suicide is actually accounted a crime; and a crime which, especially under the vulgar bigotry that prevails in England, is followed by an ignominious burial.

This legal condemnation therefore exposes a stark contradiction: society claims authority over a life whose innermost title belongs solely to the sufferer, thereby prolonging agony under the pretext of order.

The result is that the very act capable of ending meaningless torment is branded disgraceful, compelling the individual to endure the will's blind persistence even when all grounds for continuing have vanished.
slides.notes: Questions criminalization on p.9.

slides.title: Moral Feeling Toward Voluntary Death
bullets: Compare the impression that would be made upon you by the news that some one you know had committed the crime of murder with your feelings when you hear that he has met a voluntary death.
In the other case you will be moved to grief and sympathy, and mingled with your thoughts will be admiration for his courage.
This instinctive admiration shows that we secretly recognize voluntary death as a rare victory over the ceaseless demands of the will, rather than a cowardly retreat from them.

The grief that accompanies our respect therefore carries a deeper implication: we mourn not only the person but the necessity that only such an extreme denial of life can restore meaning to unbearable suffering.
slides.notes: Distinguishes suicide from crime on p.9.

slides.title: No Biblical Authority Against Suicide
bullets: Neither in the Old nor in the New Testament is there to be found any prohibition or positive disapproval of it.
The clergy have no Biblical authority to boast of, as justifying their condemnation of suicide.

This scriptural silence therefore shifts the entire burden of judgment onto the individual conscience, where the decision to end life confronts the raw fact of unrelieved suffering without any borrowed authority from revelation.

As a result the church's repeated condemnations stand exposed as human additions that cannot claim divine warrant, leaving those in despair free to weigh suicide against the deeper demand that the will be denied rather than merely extinguished.
slides.notes: Removes religious ground on p.9.

slides.title: Ancient Acceptance of Voluntary Death
bullets: Pliny says: Life is not so desirable a thing as to be protracted at any cost.
In Massilia and on the isle of Ceos, the man who could give valid reasons for relinquishing his life was handed the cup of hemlock by the magistrate.

Such customs treated the ending of life as a rational judgment that suffering had already severed the will from any further purpose, sparing the individual the indignity of being dragged onward by mere animal persistence.

By requiring public reasons before granting the means, these practices made clear that voluntary death could itself become an act of meaning, one that restored order to a life the will no longer endorsed.
slides.notes: Historical counterpoint on p.9.

slides.title: Stoic Praise of Noble Suicide
bullets: The Stoics actually praised suicide as a noble and heroic action, as hundreds of passages show.
The good man should flee life when his misfortunes become too great; the bad man, also, when he is too prosperous.
In this light suicide appears not as despair but as the final rational verdict that continued existence would only multiply pointless suffering without any prospect of restored equilibrium.
Yet the same doctrine quietly concedes that the will to live is not absolute; it may be revoked once external fortune has rendered virtue either impossible or superfluous.
slides.notes: Stoic view on p.9.

slides.title: The Will as Common Foundation
bullets: Will, as the thing-in-itself, is the foundation of all being; it is part and parcel of every creature, and the permanent element in everything.
Will, then, is that which we possess in common with all men, nay, with all animals, and even with lower forms of existence.

Because this single will animates every living thing without distinction, the blind urge that drives one creature into conflict or hunger is identical in nature to the urge felt by every other, so that private torment always echoes a universal restlessness.

Recognizing the will as our common foundation therefore exposes the illusion of radical separateness, implying that genuine compassion or the denial of willing must arise from seeing through individuality to the shared striving that binds all existence together.
slides.notes: Links will to universal suffering on p.14.

slides.title: Intellect as the Differentiating Element
bullets: That which places one being over another, and sets differences between man and man, is intellect and knowledge.
In every manifestation of self we should, as far as possible, give play to the intellect alone.
When intellect rules our outward bearing it lifts certain individuals above the herd, granting them a vantage from which the ceaseless striving of the will appears not as destiny but as spectacle.

This vantage, however, carries its own burden: the clearer the intellect, the more sharply it registers the vanity of every personal claim, making denial of the will not an abstract doctrine but the natural consequence of sustained seeing.
slides.notes: Points toward denial via intellect on p.14.

slides.title: Violent Will as Vulgar
bullets: Every violent exhibition of will is common and vulgar; in other words, it reduces us to the level of the species.
Every fit of anger is something common—every unrestrained display of joy, or of hate, or fear.
Such outbursts therefore erase the distance between one person and another, leaving only the shared, unthinking agitation of the species and extinguishing any claim to personal distinction.

The result is that every violent surge of feeling, whether hostile or ecstatic, merely feeds the same blind striving whose endless recurrence is the true source of suffering and bars the path to its denial.
slides.notes: Shows how unchecked will perpetuates suffering on p.14.

slides.title: Denial Requires Suppressing the Will
bullets: The will is the common part of us; therefore in every manifestation of self we should give play to the intellect alone.
Only by letting intellect dominate can we rise above the generic suffering that belongs to the species.

Yet this rule of the intellect cannot be won once and for all; every fresh stirring of desire tests whether the individual will truly yield or merely pretend to retreat, leaving a lasting tension between momentary clarity and the pull of renewed attachment.
When intellect holds sway the person ceases to add fresh fuel to the common misery, yet must accept the loneliness of standing apart from the very impulses that once gave life its restless warmth.
slides.notes: Connects intellect to possible denial on p.14.

slides.title: Meaning Found Only in Recognizing Suffering
bullets: Misfortune in general is the rule, and each separate misfortune seems exceptional only because we fail to see the pattern.
The meaning of existence becomes clear once we accept that suffering is its positive and necessary character.

Once this pattern of universal misfortune is grasped, the individual will encounters its own ceaseless striving as the true source of torment and begins, however reluctantly, to recoil from further willing.
Such recoil does not promise escape from pain but discloses meaning precisely in the quieting of desire, because only then does life cease to be an endless flight from the suffering it cannot avoid.
slides.notes: Synthesizes thesis on p.1.

slides.title: No Escape Through Wish-Fulfillment
bullets: If all wishes were fulfilled as soon as they arose, men would still confront the misery of boredom or the folly of arrogance.
Suffering, in measured form, alone keeps the vessel of life on course.
Even when every immediate craving is granted, the underlying drive of the will simply shifts its aim, leaving the same restless pressure that no external satisfaction can dissolve.

Thus the person who expects lasting peace from fulfilled wishes discovers instead a new torment: either the dull ache of having nothing left to pursue or the restless arrogance that demands ever greater proofs of superiority.
slides.notes: Closes the argument against optimism on p.1.
